title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Textiles Limited & Anr.
court: High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was carried out without following the principles of natural justice as no proper show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal
The domestic enquiry conducted by the respondent company was vitiated as the Enquiry Officer was biased and the petitioner was denied the right to cross-examine witnesses
The alleged misconduct of habitual absenteeism was not substantiated by credible documentary evidence and the petitioner had valid medical leave records
The respondent employer violated Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 by failing to pay retrenchment compensation and requisite notice pay
Continuous service of over twelve years entitles the petitioner to reinstatement with full back wages under the provisions of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Allahabad allowed the writ petition filed by the workman Ramesh Kumar Sharma challenging his termination from service by Hindustan Textiles Limited. The Court held that the domestic enquiry was fundamentally flawed, the principles of natural justice were violated, and the termination was illegal. The respondent company was directed to reinstate the petitioner with 75% back wages from the date of termination along with all consequential service benefits.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD

Writ Petition No. 14782 of 2022

Ramesh Kumar Sharma ... Petitioner
versus
Hindustan Textiles Limited & Anr. ... Respondents

Hon'ble Mr. Justice Sunil Kumar Verma

JUDGMENT

Date of Judgment: 14th March, 2023

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 19.08.2022 passed by the Central Government Industrial Tribunal, Kanpur, and further challenging the order of termination dated 03.01.2020 passed by the respondent company against the petitioner.

2. The petitioner was employed as a Senior Loom Operator with Hindustan Textiles Limited since 15th February, 2008. He was terminated from service on the ground of habitual absenteeism and alleged insubordination vide order dated 03.01.2020, after a domestic enquiry was purportedly conducted against him.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Shri Anil Mishra, has strenuously argued that the entire proceedings of the domestic enquiry were conducted in gross violation of the principles of natural justice. It is submitted that the Enquiry Officer appointed by the management had previously filed complaints against the petitioner, rendering him inherently biased. Furthermore, the petitioner was denied the opportunity to cross-examine the management's witnesses, which is a fundamental right in any quasi-judicial proceeding.

4. It has further been urged that the petitioner had remained absent from duty only on account of a serious medical ailment, duly certified by a registered medical practitioner, and all leave applications were submitted in accordance with the standing orders of the company. The charge of habitual absenteeism, therefore, is not borne out by any reliable evidence on record.

5. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent company, Shri Deepak Agarwal, has submitted that the enquiry was conducted fairly, the petitioner was afforded full opportunity to present his case, and the findings of the Enquiry Officer are based on cogent material. It is argued that the Industrial Tribunal correctly upheld the termination.

6. I have carefully considered the rival submissions and perused the record.

7. On a thorough examination of the enquiry proceedings, this Court is of the considered opinion that the domestic enquiry was fundamentally flawed. The Enquiry Officer, who had himself raised complaints against the petitioner on two prior occasions, was manifestly disqualified from conducting an impartial enquiry. The rule against bias is a cardinal principle of natural justice, and its violation strikes at the root of the entire proceedings. Reliance is placed upon the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Union of India v. Tulsiram Patel, (1985) 3 SCC 398.

8. Furthermore, the record reveals that the petitioner's request to cross-examine the witnesses produced by the management was summarily rejected without any valid reason. Denial of cross-examination in a disciplinary proceeding, where the charged employee's livelihood is at stake, amounts to a serious infraction of natural justice.

9. This Court also notes that the petitioner had submitted duly authenticated medical certificates for the period of absence and the same were not rebutted by cogent counter-evidence. The charge of habitual absenteeism, therefore, cannot be sustained.

10. Additionally, it is an admitted position that the petitioner had rendered continuous service for a period exceeding twelve years. No retrenchment compensation or notice pay was tendered at the time of termination, which is a mandatory requirement under Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, rendering the termination independently void.

11. In view of the foregoing, the impugned award of the Industrial Tribunal is set aside. The termination of the petitioner dated 03.01.2020 is declared illegal and void. The respondent company is directed to reinstate the petitioner forthwith in his original post with continuity of service and to pay him 75% of back wages from the date of termination within a period of three months from today.

12. The writ petition is accordingly allowed. No order as to costs.

(Justice Sunil Kumar Verma)
High Court, Allahabad
14.03.2023
